title: St. James the Apostle, Son of Zebedee
narrative:
On the twenty-fifth day of Hamle, the Ethiopian Church honours the Holy Apostle James, son of Zebedee and brother of John the Evangelist — the first of the Twelve to be martyred for the faith, beheaded by Herod Agrippa in Jerusalem around the year 44 AD. James is one of the three disciples who formed the inner circle of the Twelve: he was present at the Transfiguration on the mountain, at the raising of Jairus's daughter, and at the agony in Gethsemane. The Lord gave him and his brother John the surname Boanerges — Sons of Thunder — suggesting a characteristic intensity that is confirmed by the gospel accounts of their request to sit at the Lord's right and left hands in glory, and their suggestion that fire be called down on an inhospitable Samaritan village.

The tradition that James eventually carried the Gospel to Spain — preserved in the medieval pilgrimage tradition of the Camino de Santiago — is not explicitly referenced in the Ethiopian Synaxarium, which focuses instead on his apostolic ministry in Jerusalem and his martyrdom there. The Ethiopian account of his death draws on the tradition preserved in Clement of Alexandria and later in Eusebius, according to which the soldier who led James to his execution was so moved by the apostle's courage and composure that he declared himself a Christian on the spot and was beheaded alongside him. This detail — the martyrdom of an unnamed soldier inspired by the witness of the apostle he was executing — is one of the Ethiopian Synaxarium's favourite narrative patterns: the double witness, the chain reaction of faith.

The Ethiopian Church's commemoration of James the son of Zebedee is also an occasion to meditate on the Lord's response to the mother of the Sons of Thunder when she asked that they sit at his right and left hands in glory. 'You do not know what you are asking. Can you drink the cup that I am going to drink?' James answered 'We can,' and the Lord said 'You will indeed drink my cup.' The cup of James was beheading in Jerusalem by Herod; it came swiftly, as the first martyrdom of the Apostolic College. The word of Christ was proved true in the most literal way. May the intercession of James the Apostle strengthen all who have answered 'We can' to the Lord's call. Amen.
